summary: A 1978 musical by Andrew Lloyd Webber and Tim Rice based on the life of Eva Peron, the First Lady of Argentina. Features iconic songs including "Don't Cry for Me, Argentina" (sung from the balcony of the Casa Rosada), "Oh What a Circus" (sung by Che Guevara at her funeral, featuring the Salve Regina), "Rainbow Tour" (about her mixed reception visiting Spain and Italy), and "You Must Love Me" (written for the 1996 Madonna film adaptation). Patti LuPone won her first Best Actress Tony for the role. The musical has been compared to Here Lies Love (about Imelda Marcos) for its portrayal of a controversial female politician.

works.name: Songs and Scenes
works.description: The musical's major songs and their contexts as they appear in quizbowl clues. "Don't Cry for Me, Argentina" is the most iconic, sung from the balcony of the Casa Rosada with the line "the truth is I never left you." "Oh What a Circus" opens the show and is sung during Eva's funeral; it was originally performed by the character Che Guevara. "Rainbow Tour" describes Eva's visits to Spain and Italy, with men hiding behind raised newspapers singing about mixed results. "You Must Love Me" was written specifically for the 1996 film adaptation starring Madonna. "A Cinema in Buenos Aires, 26 July 1952" features a cinema screening interrupted by a declaration that Eva "entered immortality." Wikipedia

works.name: Production and Cast
works.description: Details about the production history and notable performers. Patti LuPone won her first Best Actress Tony for playing Eva Peron, and later went on to play Joanne in a London revival of Sondheim's Company. Madonna starred in the 1996 film adaptation. The musical was co-written by Andrew Lloyd Webber and Tim Rice. It has been compared to Here Lies Love (about Imelda Marcos, 2023 Broadway premiere) for its treatment of a controversial female politician.

works.name: Historical Context (Eva Peron)
works.description: Historical details about Eva Peron that frequently appear in clues about both the musical and the historical figure, providing useful context. Eva coined the term 'descamisados' (shirtless ones) for her husband Juan Peron's supporters. She secured women's suffrage in Argentina. She was encouraged to run for Vice President at the 'Cabildo Abierto' rally but declined. Her body was embalmed by Dr. Pedro Ara and secretly buried in Milan after the Revolucion Libertadora. She met Pope Pius XII during the Rainbow Tour and was pelted with tomatoes after meeting Franco.

comprehensive_summary:
The musical Evita by Andrew Lloyd Webber and Tim Rice appears in quizbowl primarily through its iconic songs and its historical subject. At the giveaway level, "Don't Cry for Me, Argentina" (sung from the Casa Rosada balcony) and the identification as an Andrew Lloyd Webber musical about Argentina's First Lady are the most common clues.

At the middle difficulty level, clues reference the opening number "Oh What a Circus" (sung by Che Guevara at Eva's funeral, featuring the Salve Regina), the "Rainbow Tour" number (men behind newspapers commenting on Eva's European visits), and Patti LuPone's Tony-winning performance. The 1996 Madonna film adaptation and the song "You Must Love Me" (written specifically for the film) also appear.

Power-level clues reference the "Waltz for Eva and Che" (where she is called 'short-sighted' for having 'no impossible dream'), "A Cinema in Buenos Aires, 26 July 1952" (a screening interrupted by a declaration that Eva 'entered immortality'), and the comparison to the 2023 musical Here Lies Love about Imelda Marcos.

Historical clues about Eva Peron that overlap with musical content include the descamisados, the Cabildo Abierto rally, the Rainbow Tour (meeting Franco and Pius XII), and the saga of her embalmed body. These historical details frequently appear alongside or adjacent to clues about the musical itself.

Note: 11 of 14 tossups and many bonuses returned by the search were about unrelated topics (levitation, maglev trains, urban renewal, language revitalization, etc.) that happened to match the search term through incidental mentions.
